A group is trying to build a system that generates explanations. They say the scoring rule matters most: prefer explanations that are hard to vary. But if the candidate search-pool is badly composed, that rule acts on the wrong raw material. Then “good” outputs may only be familiar fragments recombined from what was already admitted.

So they divide possible inputs into three pools: background, universal, and novel. Background contains established ideas and standard fragments. Universal contains very general explanatory resources. Novel contains guesses not yet consolidated. The warning is that, if these are mixed incorrectly, the system launders familiarity into apparent originality. It never exits the basin of known ideas; it only returns polished rearrangements.

Now apply the second premise. Early hypotheses that later become deep explanations are often easy to vary at first. So if the system enforces Deutschian rigidity too early, it suppresses the very contents of the novel pool that might eventually mature into hard-to-vary explanations. Meanwhile, background fragments are already structured enough to survive selection. The result is predictable: the search process favors regurgitation while claiming to reward explanatory strength.

In that world, novelty and regurgitation differ less by the evaluator and more by admissions control. What gets into the candidate space determines almost everything. If the novel pool is underweighted, contaminated, or judged by standards appropriate only after consolidation, the machine cannot genuinely discover. It can only synthesize plausible derivatives of what it already knows.

Now consider the social scene around this system. War has been abolished; some people fainted at that event. Later, the system produces a truce, and this amazes those same people. That reaction is intelligible if “truce” is now not a practical settlement between active wars but an explanatory or symbolic output assembled from inherited conflict-language. Since war is abolished, a truce should be obsolete. Yet the system still emits one, because its pool still contains background fragments from the old conceptual regime. Those who fainted at abolition are amazed because the abolished category still reappears in laundered form. The system has not left the old basin; it has recombined remnants of war-thinking into an apparently new achievement.

Finally, shyness makes the joyful monitor seem like an abstract treasury. The monitor is presumably observing or overseeing this process. Because of shyness, its joyful affect does not present as direct enthusiasm or confidence. Instead it appears distant, impersonal, and hoarded—like a treasury, and abstract rather than social. So even when the monitor is pleased by the produced “truce,” observers may misread both the output and the overseer: they see impersonal value where there is merely inhibited joy, and novelty where there is merely disciplined recycling.

The collision of premises yields a world where miscomposition of search-pools causes conceptual recycling; premature rigidity blocks real novelty; abolished categories persist as outputs under new labels; and the emotional stance of oversight is misperceived as abstract authority rather than shy delight.
